title: Appendicitis
oneLiner: Acute inflammation of appendix; common in young adults; diagnosis can be tricky – delay causes perforation and peritonitis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Perforated appendicitis with sepsis** – look for **generalised peritonitis** (board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, NEWS2 ≥5) → sepsis bundle + surgical referral.
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** (in any woman of reproductive age) – **always do a urinary β-hCG** before accepting a diagnosis of atypical appendicitis.  
- **Mesenteric ischaemia** (elderly / AF) – pain out of proportion, metabolic acidosis, lactate >4 mmol/L → urgent CT angiogram.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Central periumbilical pain that migrates to the right iliac fossa (RIF) over 6–24 hours  
  - **Anorexia** (95% sensitive) – ask “Have you eaten anything today?”  
  - Nausea/vomiting (often after pain onset)  
  - Low-grade pyrexia (37.5–38.5°C)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Maximal tenderness at McBurney’s point  
  - **Rovsing’s sign** (pain in RIF on left-sided palpation)  
  - **Psoas sign** (pain on passive extension of right hip) – suggests retrocaecal appendix  
  - Rebound tenderness / guarding (peritoneal irritation)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators**  
  - **Gastroenteritis** – diarrhoea pre-dates pain, vomiting more prominent, pain less localised, no anorexia.  
  - **Mesenteric adenitis** – children / young adults; recent URTI; tenderness less focal; lymph nodes on USS.  
  - **Ovarian cyst / torsion** – sudden onset; pelvic tenderness; adnexal mass on examination or USS.  
  - **Ectopic pregnancy** – syncope, shoulder tip pain, cervical excitation.  
  - **Urinary tract infection / ureteric colic** – dysuria, haematuria, tenderness in renal angle.  
- **Splitter:** **Anorexia + migration of pain** → strongly favours appendicitis over gastroenteritis. If patient is hungry and pain never migrated, reconsider.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - **Urine dip** – to exclude UTI and check for blood (stones); **always do pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age.  
  - **Temperature, HR, BP, NEWS2** – initiate sepsis screen if NEWS2 ≥5.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&Es, CRP, blood cultures (if febrile / septic).  
  - **Lactate** – if >2 mmol/L recheck and escalate; >4 mmol/L = critical illness.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** USS (especially children and young adults) – visualises appendix diameter >6 mm, wall thickness, free fluid.  
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (gold standard in adults if USS equivocal or age >50). *Note: in pregnancy, MRI or low-dose CT after discussion with radiology.*  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Alvarado Score** (MANTRELS):  
    - Migration of pain (1), Anorexia (1), Nausea/vomiting (1), Tenderness in RIF (2), Rebound pain (1), Elevated temperature (1), Leucocytosis (2), Left shift (1)  
    - Score ≤3: low risk; 4–6: equivocal; ≥7: high risk – proceed to imaging or surgical review.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Urinary β-hCG** in any woman aged 12–55. Omission can be fatal.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Analgesia** – do not withhold in suspected appendicitis (NICE recommends paracetamol +/- morphine while awaiting assessment).  
  - IV fluids if tachycardic or NBM.  
  - **Nil by mouth** (NBM) – for potential surgery.  
  - **Sepsis bundle** if NEWS2 ≥5 or suspected perforation: IV antibiotics, blood cultures, lactate, fluid bolus.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - **Surgical review** – if high probability (Alvarado ≥7, positive imaging).  
  - **Conservative management** (uncomplicated appendicitis in selected patients) – IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS, or [check local policy]) for 24–48h, then oral switch. Failure = surgery.  
  - Post-op: early mobilisation, thromboprophylaxis, wound care.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable – surgery or antibiotic course completed as inpatient.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride ± 5% dextrose – rate 30–50 mL/kg over 24h (adjust for renal function).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless high bleeding risk).  
  - **Diet:** NBM until surgical plan clear; if conservative, start clear fluids then build after 24h.  
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly (or per NEWS2 protocol) – escalate for NEWS ≥5.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (antibiotics for peritonitis / conservative):**  
  - *Co-amoxiclav* 1.2 g IV TDS (or 500 mg/125 mg oral TDS if clinically improving).  
  - Alternative (penicillin allergy): *Gentamicin* 5 mg/kg IV once daily (check levels pre-second dose; adjust renal function) + *Metronidazole* 500 mg IV TDS.  
- **Common alternative:** *Cefuroxime* 1.5 g IV TDS + *Metronidazole* 500 mg IV TDS.  
- **Key contraindications:** Co-amoxiclav – history of penicillin allergy; Gentamicin – renal impairment (use with caution, check pre-dose levels).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Gentamicin requires creatinine clearance estimation; reduce dose or extend interval if eGFR <30.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin – forgetting to check trough level before second dose** (risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity). Always check the first level at 18–24h post-dose.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Perforation (risk increases after 36h of symptoms) → generalised peritonitis, sepsis, abscess.  
  - Appendix mass / abscess – may require percutaneous drainage.  
  - Wound infection (especially if perforated).  
  - ileus (post-op).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Intra-abdominal adhesions → future obstruction (rare).  
  - Stump appendicitis (if retained appendix tip).
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality** <0.1% (uncomplicated) but rises to 5% if perforation with sepsis.  
- **Full recovery** in 1–2 weeks post-op. Conservative patients may recur (up to 30% at 1 year).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do examine the right hip** (psoas sign) – quick to do, high specificity for retrocaecal appendix.  
- **Use the Alvarado score** in the ED – it’s fast and helps you communicate risk to the surgeon.  
- **Always check a pregnancy test** before the patient leaves your care – even if she says she can’t be pregnant.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Withholding analgesia** – does not mask signs; NICE recommends giving it.  
- **Giving oral antibiotics** if patient is vomiting or has peritonitis – must be IV.  
- **Discharging a patient with normal CRP and WBC but high clinical suspicion** – up to 10% of patients have normal inflammatory markers, especially early on.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Pain worsens or spreads to whole abdomen  
  - Vomiting persists (cannot keep fluids down)  
  - Fever ≥38°C or rigors  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - If imaging equivocal and symptoms resolving – GP review in 48h.  
  - If conservative management – surgical clinic in 2 weeks to discuss interval appendicectomy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (ICU / crash team) if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 despite fluid resuscitation  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - GCS <15 or hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)  
  - Clinical signs of peritonitis (rigidity, absent bowel sounds) – urgent surgical review.  
- **Specific thresholds for surgical referral:**  
  - Alvarado ≥7  
  - Positive imaging (USS/CT)  
  - Any woman of childbearing age with negative pregnancy test but high suspicion – discuss with gynaecology.

rawMarkdown:
## Appendicitis
**One-liner:** Acute inflammation of appendix; common in young adults; diagnosis can be tricky – delay causes perforation and peritonitis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Perforated appendicitis with sepsis** – look for **generalised peritonitis** (board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, NEWS2 ≥5) → sepsis bundle + surgical referral.
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** (in any woman of reproductive age) – **always do a urinary β-hCG** before accepting a diagnosis of atypical appendicitis.  
- **Mesenteric ischaemia** (elderly / AF) – pain out of proportion, metabolic acidosis, lactate >4 mmol/L → urgent CT angiogram.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Central periumbilical pain that migrates to the right iliac fossa (RIF) over 6–24 hours  
  - **Anorexia** (95% sensitive) – ask “Have you eaten anything today?”  
  - Nausea/vomiting (often after pain onset)  
  - Low-grade pyrexia (37.5–38.5°C)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Maximal tenderness at McBurney’s point  
  - **Rovsing’s sign** (pain in RIF on left-sided palpation)  
  - **Psoas sign** (pain on passive extension of right hip) – suggests retrocaecal appendix  
  - Rebound tenderness / guarding (peritoneal irritation)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators**  
  - **Gastroenteritis** – diarrhoea pre-dates pain, vomiting more prominent, pain less localised, no anorexia.  
  - **Mesenteric adenitis** – children / young adults; recent URTI; tenderness less focal; lymph nodes on USS.  
  - **Ovarian cyst / torsion** – sudden onset; pelvic tenderness; adnexal mass on examination or USS.  
  - **Ectopic pregnancy** – syncope, shoulder tip pain, cervical excitation.  
  - **Urinary tract infection / ureteric colic** – dysuria, haematuria, tenderness in renal angle.  
- **Splitter:** **Anorexia + migration of pain** → strongly favours appendicitis over gastroenteritis. If patient is hungry and pain never migrated, reconsider.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - **Urine dip** – to exclude UTI and check for blood (stones); **always do pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age.  
  - **Temperature, HR, BP, NEWS2** – initiate sepsis screen if NEWS2 ≥5.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&Es, CRP, blood cultures (if febrile / septic).  
  - **Lactate** – if >2 mmol/L recheck and escalate; >4 mmol/L = critical illness.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** USS (especially children and young adults) – visualises appendix diameter >6 mm, wall thickness, free fluid.  
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (gold standard in adults if USS equivocal or age >50). *Note: in pregnancy, MRI or low-dose CT after discussion with radiology.*  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Alvarado Score** (MANTRELS):  
    - Migration of pain (1), Anorexia (1), Nausea/vomiting (1), Tenderness in RIF (2), Rebound pain (1), Elevated temperature (1), Leucocytosis (2), Left shift (1)  
    - Score ≤3: low risk; 4–6: equivocal; ≥7: high risk – proceed to imaging or surgical review.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Urinary β-hCG** in any woman aged 12–55. Omission can be fatal.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Analgesia** – do not withhold in suspected appendicitis (NICE recommends paracetamol +/- morphine while awaiting assessment).  
  - IV fluids if tachycardic or NBM.  
  - **Nil by mouth** (NBM) – for potential surgery.  
  - **Sepsis bundle** if NEWS2 ≥5 or suspected perforation: IV antibiotics, blood cultures, lactate, fluid bolus.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - **Surgical review** – if high probability (Alvarado ≥7, positive imaging).  
  - **Conservative management** (uncomplicated appendicitis in selected patients) – IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS, or [check local policy]) for 24–48h, then oral switch. Failure = surgery.  
  - Post-op: early mobilisation, thromboprophylaxis, wound care.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Not applicable – surgery or antibiotic course completed as inpatient.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride ± 5% dextrose – rate 30–50 mL/kg over 24h (adjust for renal function).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless high bleeding risk).  
  - **Diet:** NBM until surgical plan clear; if conservative, start clear fluids then build after 24h.  
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly (or per NEWS2 protocol) – escalate for NEWS ≥5.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (antibiotics for peritonitis / conservative):**  
  - *Co-amoxiclav* 1.2 g IV TDS (or 500 mg/125 mg oral TDS if clinically improving).  
  - Alternative (penicillin allergy): *Gentamicin* 5 mg/kg IV once daily (check levels pre-second dose; adjust renal function) + *Metronidazole* 500 mg IV TDS.  
- **Common alternative:** *Cefuroxime* 1.5 g IV TDS + *Metronidazole* 500 mg IV TDS.  
- **Key contraindications:** Co-amoxiclav – history of penicillin allergy; Gentamicin – renal impairment (use with caution, check pre-dose levels).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Gentamicin requires creatinine clearance estimation; reduce dose or extend interval if eGFR <30.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin – forgetting to check trough level before second dose** (risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity). Always check the first level at 18–24h post-dose.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Perforation (risk increases after 36h of symptoms) → generalised peritonitis, sepsis, abscess.  
  - Appendix mass / abscess – may require percutaneous drainage.  
  - Wound infection (especially if perforated).  
  - ileus (post-op).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Intra-abdominal adhesions → future obstruction (rare).  
  - Stump appendicitis (if retained appendix tip).

### Prognosis
- **Mortality** <0.1% (uncomplicated) but rises to 5% if perforation with sepsis.  
- **Full recovery** in 1–2 weeks post-op. Conservative patients may recur (up to 30% at 1 year).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do examine the right hip** (psoas sign) – quick to do, high specificity for retrocaecal appendix.  
- **Use the Alvarado score** in the ED – it’s fast and helps you communicate risk to the surgeon.  
- **Always check a pregnancy test** before the patient leaves your care – even if she says she can’t be pregnant.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Withholding analgesia** – does not mask signs; NICE recommends giving it.  
- **Giving oral antibiotics** if patient is vomiting or has peritonitis – must be IV.  
- **Discharging a patient with normal CRP and WBC but high clinical suspicion** – up to 10% of patients have normal inflammatory markers, especially early on.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Pain worsens or spreads to whole abdomen  
  - Vomiting persists (cannot keep fluids down)  
  - Fever ≥38°C or rigors  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - If imaging equivocal and symptoms resolving – GP review in 48h.  
  - If conservative management – surgical clinic in 2 weeks to discuss interval appendicectomy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (ICU / crash team) if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 despite fluid resuscitation  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - GCS <15 or hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)  
  - Clinical signs of peritonitis (rigidity, absent bowel sounds) – urgent surgical review.  
- **Specific thresholds for surgical referral:**  
  - Alvarado ≥7  
  - Positive imaging (USS/CT)  
  - Any woman of childbearing age with negative pregnancy test but high suspicion – discuss with gynaecology.
